adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure the organisation's regulatory compliance program reflects the May 21, 2026 effective date of the Rule 202.5(e) rescission, updating all SEC enforcement settlement policies, procedures, and training materials accordingly.

Maintain an accurate and current inventory of existing SEC consent orders and settlement agreements whose embedded no-deny clauses are now unenforced by Commission policy, enabling informed decisions on post-settlement public communications.

Define and enforce internal protocols governing post-settlement public statements to ensure that any denial of SEC enforcement allegations, now permissible under the rescission, complies with the residual prohibition on false or misleading statements under federal securities law.

Ensure that external reporting and investor communications accurately characterise the organisation's enforcement settlement status in light of the Rule 202.5(e) rescission, avoiding representations that could constitute misleading omissions about the basis for settlement.

Assess whether changed post-settlement communications rights under the May 2026 rescission create material contingent liabilities or require updated financial-statement disclosures related to existing or anticipated SEC enforcement settlements.

graded_claims.claim: The SEC adopted a final rule rescinding Rule 202.5(e) on May 18, 2026.
The rule was published in the Federal Register and became effective May 21, 2026.
Rule 202.5(e) had been in place since 1972.
The Commission will not enforce existing no-deny provisions in settlements already entered.
The rescission was championed by SEC Chairman Paul Atkins.
The SEC concluded that the rescission is a rule of agency procedure under the APA and therefore did not require notice-and-comment.
Post-settlement public statements must still comply with securities laws and must not be false or misleading.
The rescission does not affect the SEC's discretion to seek admissions as part of a settlement.
The rescission aligns the SEC with the overwhelming majority of federal agencies, including the DOJ, that do not have a comparable no-deny policy.
The change may have profound implications for settlement strategy and post-settlement messaging to relevant constituencies.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Amend the organisation's written SEC enforcement-response policy to
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The General Counsel must, by June 30, 2026, identify and redline every provision in the organisation's written SEC enforcement-response policy that imposes an internal no-deny undertaking mirroring the now-rescinded Rule 202.5(e) — which became legally inoperative effective May 21, 2026 — and issue a policy amendment removing or replacing such provisions with language reflecting the restored right to contest allegations subject to securities-law constraints. The amended policy must be distributed to the Legal, Compliance, and Communications teams via a dated memorandum, with each team's head providing written acknowledgement of receipt; the executed memoranda constitute evidence of completion.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires a policy update; this adaptation specifies the exact regulatory anchor (Rule 202.5(e), effective May 21, 2026), the precise internal provisions at risk (those that internalised the no-deny obligation beyond what the SEC now requires), the accountability chain (General Counsel), and the concrete evidence artefact (dated acknowledgement memoranda).

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Conduct a full inventory of existing SEC consent
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Legal/Compliance function must, within 30 days of May 21, 2026, complete a documented inventory of every SEC consent order, administrative settlement, and deferred-prosecution-equivalent agreement to which the organisation is a party, flagging each instrument that contains an explicit no-deny clause; for each flagged instrument, produce a one-page legal memorandum confirming that the Commission declared such clauses unenforceable by operation of the final rule effective May 21, 2026, and stating the full scope of public-statement rights now available in that matter. The completed inventory and supporting memoranda must be reviewed and signed off by the Chief Legal Officer and retained in the organisation's regulatory-matters repository as the evidentiary record.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control calls for an inventory; this adaptation anchors it to the specific Commission declaration of non-enforcement of existing no-deny provisions, imposes a 30-day deadline measured from the rule's effective date, and requires per-matter legal memoranda rather than a summary list, capturing the nuanced variation in communications rights across an existing settlement portfolio.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Update regulatory-change monitoring procedures to track any court
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Regulatory Affairs function must, by June 4, 2026, add a standing watch item to its regulatory-change monitoring calendar specifically tracking federal court filings that challenge the SEC's determination — confirmed in the May 18, 2026 adopting release — that rescission of Rule 202.5(e) constitutes a rule of agency procedure exempt from APA notice-and-comment; the watch item must specify that any complaint filed, any motion for a temporary restraining order, or any judicial stay issued constitutes a Priority 1 alert requiring immediate notification to the General Counsel and Chief Compliance Officer within one business day of discovery. Monitoring must cover at minimum PACER full-text filings and legal-news services, and a nil-finding report must be filed monthly until any challenge is resolved or the monitoring period is formally closed by the General Counsel.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires tracking APA challenges; this adaptation specifies the precise legal theory being monitored (the SEC's APA-exempt characterisation confirmed in the adopting release), the exact alert threshold and escalation path, the specific monitoring channels, and introduces a nil-finding reporting obligation to prevent monitoring from lapsing silently.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Revise employee and officer training on SEC enforcement
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Chief Compliance Officer must commission and deploy, no later than August 31, 2026, a revised training module for all officers, directors, and employees with responsibility for SEC enforcement matters or public disclosures, covering: (a) the post-May 21, 2026 right to publicly contest allegations in SEC enforcement settlements and the procedural history of the rescinded Rule 202.5(e); (b) the confirmed continuing obligations under Exchange Act Rule 10b-5 and the Securities Act anti-fraud provisions, which render post-settlement statements false or misleading impermissible regardless of the rescission; and (c) illustrative fact patterns distinguishing permissible factual denials from statements that could constitute actionable misrepresentations. Completion must be tracked in the organisation's learning-management system, with a target of 100% completion for in-scope personnel within 30 days of module deployment; completion records serve as the evidentiary artefact.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires updated training; this adaptation pairs the new permissive right with its specific residual legal constraints (Rule 10b-5, Securities Act anti-fraud), sets a concrete deadline tied to the rule's effective date, and requires illustrative fact patterns to address the practical ambiguity employees face when deciding whether a post-settlement denial crosses the line into an actionable misstatement.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish a pre-clearance protocol for any public statement
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The General Counsel must, by July 15, 2026, establish and publish internally a pre-clearance protocol requiring that any proposed public statement — including press releases, earnings-call prepared remarks, regulatory filings, social-media posts, and interviews — that contests, qualifies, or comments on allegations in any prior or pending SEC enforcement settlement be reviewed and approved in writing by outside securities counsel and the Chief Legal Officer before release; the protocol must specify a maximum 5-business-day review turnaround, identify an expedited 48-hour track for time-sensitive disclosures, and require that each cleared statement include a documented legal sign-off memorandum addressing compliance with Rule 10b-5 and the Securities Act anti-fraud provisions confirmed as continuing obligations under the May 18, 2026 adopting release. The protocol document and each individual sign-off memorandum constitute the evidentiary record.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control calls for a pre-clearance protocol; this adaptation specifies the exact statement types covered, the dual-approver structure (outside counsel plus CLO), binding turnaround timelines, an expedited track for urgent disclosures, and ties the legal-sufficiency standard directly to the confirmed continuing obligations identified in the SEC's own adopting release.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Assess whether the organisation's existing D&O and E&O
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Risk or Finance function, in coordination with outside insurance counsel, must, by July 31, 2026, obtain and document a written assessment from the organisation's D&O and E&O insurers confirming whether: (a) the exercise of post-settlement public denial rights — newly permissible following the Rule 202.5(e) rescission effective May 21, 2026 — constitutes a circumstance requiring prior notice under the relevant policies; (b) any endorsement or coverage adjustment is needed to address litigation exposure arising from third-party reliance on post-settlement public denials, a risk category not present under the prior regime; and (c) existing policy exclusions for regulatory admissions or consent-order conduct require re-evaluation in light of the Commission's confirmed retained discretion to seek admissions as part of settlements. The written insurer responses and any resulting endorsements or reservation-of-rights letters constitute the evidentiary artefact.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires insurance notification review; this adaptation operationalises the specific new litigation exposure vector (third-party reliance on post-settlement denials), ties it to the confirmed retained admissions discretion that could produce asymmetric coverage scenarios, and requires written insurer responses rather than internal assessment alone.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Evaluate whether FINRA AWC settlement counterparts to Rule 202
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Chief Compliance Officer, in coordination with FINRA regulatory counsel, must, by August 15, 2026, produce a written comparative analysis determining whether FINRA Acceptance, Waiver & Consent (AWC) settlements to which the organisation is a party contain no-deny provisions that remain contractually and regulatorily binding notwithstanding the May 21, 2026 Rule 202.5(e) rescission, given that the rescission explicitly aligns the SEC with other federal agencies but does not bind FINRA as a self-regulatory organisation; for any matter where the organisation holds both an SEC settlement (now no-deny-free) and a FINRA AWC covering overlapping conduct, the analysis must identify whether differential treatment between the two instruments creates inconsistent disclosure obligations for dually-registered broker-dealer personnel or entities. The analysis must be reviewed by the Chief Legal Officer, and a remediation or monitoring plan must be appended where differential obligations are identified.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control flags the FINRA/SEC divergence question; this adaptation specifies the AWC as the relevant FINRA instrument, identifies the specific risk scenario (overlapping conduct covered by both an SEC settlement and an AWC), grounds the analysis in the confirmed SEC-federal-agency alignment statement in the adopting release, and requires a remediation plan where inconsistencies are found rather than stopping at identification.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Review the organisation's crisis-communications and investor-relations playbook to
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Head of Communications and the General Counsel must jointly, by July 31, 2026, revise the organisation's crisis-communications and investor-relations playbook to incorporate a decision-tree that governs post-settlement public statements, with the tree bifurcating on the settlement date relative to May 21, 2026: for pre-rescission settlements, the decision-tree must confirm that no-deny provisions are unenforceable by Commission declaration but that securities-law anti-fraud constraints remain, and route all contemplated public statements to the pre-clearance protocol established under the organisation's Rule 202.5(e) rescission response; for post-rescission settlements, the tree must address the absence of any no-deny constraint while applying the same anti-fraud routing. The revised playbook must be reviewed and formally adopted by the Chief Executive Officer and distributed to IR, Legal, and Communications teams, with dated distribution receipts as evidence of completion.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires a playbook update; this adaptation builds a specific bifurcated decision architecture around the confirmed pre/post-May 21 settlement date distinction, integrates the pre-clearance protocol from the organisation's broader rescission response, and assigns joint accountability between Communications and Legal to prevent the two functions from operating inconsistent guidance.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Brief the Board and Audit Committee on the Rule 202
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The General Counsel must, by the earlier of the next regularly scheduled Board meeting or July 31, 2026, present to the Board and Audit Committee a written briefing covering: (a) the full portfolio of existing SEC enforcement settlements to which the organisation is a party, summarising the allegations in each and identifying any where management is considering exercising the newly restored right to make public statements contesting those allegations; (b) a legal opinion from outside securities counsel addressing whether any such contemplated denial, if made publicly, could constitute an actionable misstatement or omission under Exchange Act Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5, given that the May 18, 2026 adopting release confirms post-settlement statements must not be false or misleading; and (c) a summary of the Commission's confirmed retained discretion to seek admissions, contextualising whether any existing settlement was entered with or without an admission. The Board must formally acknowledge receipt and direct management on any matters requiring immediate escalation; Board minutes recording that acknowledgement are the evidentiary artefact.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires a Board briefing; this adaptation specifies the three-part structure of the briefing, mandates an external legal opinion rather than internal counsel alone, and ties the actionable-misstatement analysis to the specific confirmed obligation in the adopting release, preventing the Board from receiving only a policy update without understanding the live exposure in the existing settlement portfolio.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Reassess the organisation's loss-contingency disclosures under ASC 450
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Chief Financial Officer must, in consultation with outside securities and accounting counsel, and prior to the issuance of any financial statements for periods ending on or after May 21, 2026, conduct a written reassessment of every SEC enforcement settlement included in the organisation's ASC 450 loss-contingency disclosures, identifying any matter where management is now considering exercising the post-rescission right to publicly deny underlying allegations; for each such matter, the reassessment must evaluate whether a contemplated public denial is consistent with the probability-of-loss assessment previously disclosed (probable, reasonably possible, or remote) and, where there is inconsistency, recommend either a disclosure revision or a documented decision to refrain from denial. The reassessment memorandum, reviewed and signed by the CFO and Chief Accounting Officer, constitutes the evidentiary artefact and must be made available to the external auditors.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control flags ASC 450 inconsistency risk; this adaptation operationalises the specific inconsistency scenario (a public denial signalling a view of loss probability different from what was disclosed), ties the review deadline to financial-statement issuance cycles for periods ending on or after the rule's effective date, and requires the output to be made available to external auditors to close the loop between the financial reporting and audit processes.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Confirm with external auditors whether the Rule 202
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: The Chief Financial Officer must, before the filing of any financial statements for periods ending on or after May 21, 2026, obtain written confirmation from the organisation's external auditors addressing whether: (a) the Rule 202.5(e) rescission itself, as a final rule effective May 21, 2026, constitutes a recognised or non-recognised subsequent event under ASC 855 that requires disclosure in the notes to those financial statements; and (b) any public statement the organisation has made or intends to make contesting prior SEC settlement allegations — now permissible under the rescission — independently requires subsequent-events disclosure on the grounds that it reflects a material change in the organisation's legal position or loss-contingency assessment. The external auditors' written response and any agreed disclosure language must be appended to the audit file and to the pre-clearance documentation required under the organisation's Rule 202.5(e) rescission response protocols.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control asks whether subsequent-events disclosure is required; this adaptation separates the two distinct disclosure triggers (the rule change itself versus any resulting public denial), anchors both to ASC 855, specifies the financial-statement filing as the mandatory decision point, and cross-references the pre-clearance protocol to prevent the financial-reporting and public-communications workstreams from reaching inconsistent conclusions about the same statement.

incidents.headline: Trend Micro Apex One zero-day CVE-2026-34926 exploited, CISA orders federal patch by June 4
incidents.summary: Trend Micro disclosed CVE-2026-34926, an actively exploited directory traversal vulnerability in the on-premises Apex One server, which allows a pre-authenticated local attacker with administrator privileges to inject malicious code and deploy it to all managed endpoint agents across an organisation. CISA added the flaw to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog and ordered Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to patch by June 4, 2026, citing the flaw as a significant risk to the federal enterprise. The vulnerability is particularly dangerous as it targets the security endpoint management platform itself, meaning exploitation can undermine the integrity of the entire enterprise endpoint protection stack.

secondary_mappings.why: CISA's mandatory KEV directive with a June 4 federal remediation deadline constitutes a binding enforcement action against all FCEB agencies running Apex One.

Apex One is an enterprise threat detection and endpoint protection product; exploitation allows attackers to weaponise the detection layer itself to deploy malicious code to all managed agents, compromising detection integrity.

Compromise of a centralised endpoint security management server can cascade across all managed devices, creating a business continuity event requiring emergency patching and potential agent quarantine.

incidents.severity_rationale: Actively exploited vulnerability in a widely deployed enterprise endpoint security management server; exploitation allows malicious code injection that propagates to all managed endpoint agents, effectively turning the security product into an attack vector with broad enterprise blast radius and a binding CISA federal remediation deadline.

sources.title: Trend Micro warns of Apex One zero-day exploited in the wild

incidents.headline: DOJ indicts CIMC, Singamas and two others for container price-fixing
incidents.summary: On May 19, 2026, the U.S. Department of Justice unsealed a superseding indictment against four of the world's largest shipping container manufacturers — CIMC, Singamas, Dong Fang, and CXIC — and seven Chinese executives for conspiring to restrict output and fix prices of standard dry shipping containers from November 2019 to at least January 2024. The four companies collectively manufacture approximately 95% of the world's standard unrefrigerated containers. The DOJ alleged the conspiracy roughly doubled container prices between 2019 and 2021 and impacted approximately USD 35 billion in global commerce, with CIMC's container manufacturing profits rising from USD 19.8 million in 2019 to USD 1.75 billion in 2021.

secondary_mappings.why: DOJ Antitrust Division criminal indictment under Sherman Act Section 1 with potential fines exceeding statutory maximums and up to 10 years imprisonment for individuals.
Artificially restricted container output created systemic supply chain disruptions for global logistics operators, shipping lines, and container lessors.
Coordinated output restriction and price-fixing inflated costs for US purchasers and global customers, constituting criminal anticompetitive conduct affecting billions in commerce.
All four indicted manufacturers are Chinese nationals or entities; the case involves cross-border criminal prosecution and extradition proceedings in France.
Indictment of companies controlling 95% of global dry container supply exposes downstream customers and supply chain partners to reputational and procurement risk.

incidents.severity_rationale: Four companies controlling ~95% of global dry container supply indicted for a four-year cartel affecting USD 35 billion in commerce; criminal penalties potential and ongoing extradition proceedings.
incidents.threat_actor: CIMC, Singamas Container Holdings, Dong Fang, CXIC (and seven named executives including Vick Nam Hing Ma, Siong Seng Teo, Boliang Mai)
incidents.financial_impact_disclosed: ~USD 35 billion in affected global commerce; CIMC container profits alleged to have risen from USD 19.8m (2019) to USD 1.75bn (2021)

graded_claims.claim: On May 19, 2026, the DOJ unsealed a superseding indictment against CIMC, Singamas, Dong Fang, and CXIC for conspiring to fix prices and restrict output of standard dry shipping containers.
The conspiracy ran from at least November 2019 through at least January 2024.
The four companies collectively manufacture approximately 95% of the world's standard unrefrigerated shipping containers.
Container prices roughly doubled between 2019 and 2021 as a result of the alleged conspiracy.
The conspiracy impacted approximately USD 35 billion in global commerce.
CIMC's container manufacturing profits rose from USD 19.8 million in 2019 to USD 1.75 billion in 2021.
Seven Chinese executives were named in the indictment, including the CEOs of CIMC (Boliang Mai), Singamas (Siong Seng Teo), and CXIC (Yuqiang Zhang).
Singamas marketing executive Vick Nam Hing Ma was arrested in Paris in April 2026 while attempting to board a flight to Hong Kong and is awaiting extradition.
Two additional unnamed co-conspirator companies (Company A and Company B) are referenced in the indictment and may be cooperating with the DOJ.
The indictment was originally filed in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California in January 2025 and kept under seal until May 2026.
Conspirators agreed to install cross-factory video surveillance to police the output-restriction agreement.
China COSCO Shipping acquired Dong Fang and the dry freight container business of Singamas in 2019.
Hong Kong-listed shares of CIMC and Singamas fell 1.5% and 1.6% respectively on the day following the unsealing.
China is likely to view the indictment as unlawful extraterritorial jurisdiction and may invoke domestic counter-legislation.
The DOJ indictment may jeopardize a potential September 2026 visit by Xi Jinping to the United States.

incidents.headline: TPI Composites wind blade affiliates Chapter 11 plan confirmed by court
incidents.summary: On May 21, 2026, a Texas bankruptcy judge approved the Chapter 11 plan of two affiliates of TPI Composites, the world's largest independent wind turbine blade manufacturer, allowing an equity sale and incorporating a settlement between secured lenders and the creditors committee. TPI Composites filed for Chapter 11 in August 2025 amid 'industry-wide pressures,' with shares falling ~95%, and had sought emergency Vendor Advance Agreements with key customer Vestas Wind Systems to maintain blade deliveries during the restructuring. The plan approval marks a critical juncture for wind turbine OEMs including Vestas and Siemens Gamesa, which are single-source dependent on TPI for a significant share of blade supply.

secondary_mappings.why: TPI's insolvency and restructuring threatened continuous wind blade supply to Vestas and Siemens Gamesa, forcing emergency vendor advance arrangements to prevent manufacturing halts.
Chapter 11 proceedings with equity sale and creditor settlement directly implicate financial exposure of OEM customers holding purchase agreements.
The plan approval involves equity transfer, requiring OEM customers to reassess strategic supplier relationships and re-qualify replacement blade manufacturers.
U.S. Trustee objected to third-party releases in the plan, citing post-Purdue Supreme Court precedent, complicating confirmation and creditor distributions.

incidents.severity_rationale: TPI controls a significant share of global independent wind blade manufacturing; its restructuring and equity transfer creates supply uncertainty for Vestas and Siemens Gamesa and may delay renewable energy projects.

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure that single-source and concentrated third-party manufacturing dependencies — specifically where a counterparty enters insolvency proceedings — are identified, stress-tested, and subject to documented continuity arrangements before a court-supervised restructuring forces reactive emergency agreements.

Maintain validated alternative sourcing or production-continuity arrangements for wind blade supply lines where the sole external manufacturer (TPI Composites) is operating under Chapter 11 debtor-in-possession constraints and court-gated asset transfers.

Ensure operational resilience plans account for multi-jurisdiction manufacturing disruption — across US (Iowa, Arizona), Mexico, and India — when a single critical supplier enters bankruptcy and asset disposals fragment the production network.

Ensure financial exposure to a bankrupt counterparty — including advance payments, Vendor Advance Agreements, and DIP-financing obligations — is quantified, disclosed, and stress-tested against recovery scenarios under the confirmed Chapter 11 plan.

Ensure strategic portfolio decisions account for single-OEM blade manufacturing dependency risk, particularly where the collapse of an independent manufacturer (TPI Composites) forces vertical integration or emergency asset acquisition to preserve supply.

Ensure compliance with court-ordered obligations, plan milestones, and automatic stay provisions under US Bankruptcy Code in all transactions with TPI Composites during and after the Chapter 11 plan confirmation period.

graded_claims.claim: A Texas bankruptcy judge approved the Chapter 11 plan of two TPI Composites affiliates on May 21, 2026.
TPI Composites filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on August 11, 2025, alongside 21 affiliated debtors.
TPI Composites accumulated $1.077 billion in liabilities against $591.7 million in assets at filing.
TPI shares fell approximately 95% following the bankruptcy filing.
TPI sought emergency Vendor Advance Agreement approval with Vestas Wind Systems on December 10, 2025, to address acute liquidity needs.
Without the Vestas Vendor Advance Agreement, TPI risked being unable to pay for materials ordered between the petition date and December 5, 2025.
Vestas is acquiring TPI's Mexican subsidiaries (TPI Mexico V LLC and TPI Mexico VI LLC) and India assets.
TPI's Turkish operations were acquired by XCS Composites (UAE) for approximately 92.9 million euros.
GE Vernova and Vestas together accounted for a majority of TPI revenue and are its two primary customers.
TPI's cash was expected to fall below $1 million by November 21, 2025, as stated in court filings.
The Chapter 11 plan incorporates a settlement between secured lenders and the creditors committee.
Siemens Gamesa is single-source dependent on TPI for a significant share of blade supply.
The 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act,' signed July 4, 2025, requires wind projects to be completed by end of 2027 or begin construction by July 4, 2026 to qualify for the Production Tax Credit.

incidents.if_you_operate_x_then_y: If you operate a wind turbine OEM or wind farm developer with outsourced blade supply from TPI Composites, you should within 24-72 hours confirm in writing with TPI's restructuring counsel and your procurement team which specific blade orders are covered by the confirmed Chapter 11 plan's continuity provisions and which facilities remain operationally funded through the equity-transfer closing date.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Assess and document TPI Composites' financial viability quarterly
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Effective immediately and retroactive to August 11, 2025, replace all standard vendor financial-health scorecard assessments for TPI Composites with a quarterly viability dossier anchored exclusively to (a) DIP-financing drawdown balances as filed in case 25-34655 (CML) in the Southern District of Texas, (b) court-filed cash-flow projections showing the sub-$1 million cash threshold that materialised by November 21, 2025, and (c) post-confirmation equity-transfer milestones for the Vestas acquisition of TPI Mexico V LLC, TPI Mexico VI LLC, and India assets. The Chief Procurement Officer must certify each quarterly dossier and route it to the CFO within 15 business days of each court-filing deadline, with the first certification due no later than 30 days after the May 21, 2026 plan confirmation date. Completion evidence is the signed dossier filed in the supplier risk management system with case-docket citation for each financial data point used.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation replaces the failed generic scorecard mechanism with court-docket-sourced, case-specific financial indicators that would have flagged TPI's insolvency trajectory — including the November 2025 sub-$1M cash event — well before the August 2025 filing, and ties the cadence to confirmed bankruptcy milestones rather than calendar quarters.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Update TPI Composites' supplier risk classification to 'Critical
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Immediately upon issuance of this control, reclassify TPI Composites and all 21 affiliated debtors in the enterprise supplier registry to 'Critical Single-Source Under Court Supervision — Case 25-34655 (CML),' and freeze execution of any new or amended supply agreement — including any successor Vendor Advance Agreement structured analogously to the December 10, 2025 emergency agreement with Vestas Wind Systems — until the board's Risk Committee provides written sign-off citing the reclassification. The Chief Supply Chain Officer must present the reclassification record and a summary of all active agreements (including the GE Vernova Long Lead Materials Agreement and any remaining Vestas advance obligations) to the board Risk Committee at its next scheduled meeting and at every subsequent meeting while case 25-34655 remains open. Completion evidence is the updated registry entry with board Risk Committee resolution number and the signed meeting minutes.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation operationalises the 'Critical Single-Source' designation by tying it explicitly to case 25-34655 and the December 2025 emergency liquidity event, preventing recurrence of advance agreements being executed at operational level without board visibility during an active insolvency proceeding.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish and test a blade-supply continuity protocol for
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 90 days of the May 21, 2026 plan confirmation, the Chief Supply Chain Officer must deliver a blade-supply continuity protocol for each of the five named TPI Composites facilities — Newton, Iowa; Scottsboro, Arizona; TPI Mexico V LLC; TPI Mexico VI LLC; and the India facility — specifying, for each site, either a named qualified alternative blade supplier with confirmed capacity or a documented internal manufacturing ramp plan, including volume, timeline, and capital requirement. Each site-level protocol must identify the minimum production-stoppage trigger (expressed in days of zero output) that activates the named alternative, and must be validated by a tabletop exercise completed within the 90-day window. Completion evidence is the signed protocol document per site, the tabletop exercise report, and confirmation that alternative suppliers or ramp plans have been formally engaged or scoped.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation moves beyond a generic continuity policy by requiring site-by-site named alternatives that reflect the actual post-confirmation asset disposition — Vestas acquiring Mexico V, VI, and India while Iowa and Arizona remain in the restructured estate — so each facility's distinct ownership and operational risk is addressed discretely.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Conduct a site-by-site continuity assessment for each TPI
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 45 days of the May 21, 2026 plan confirmation, the Operations Continuity Director must complete a site-by-site inventory buffer assessment for Newton (Iowa), Scottsboro (Arizona), TPI Mexico V LLC, TPI Mexico VI LLC, and the India facility, quantifying the minimum blade inventory (by turbine model and OEM customer — Vestas and GE Vernova) required to sustain each OEM's turbine assembly schedules through the full equity-transfer window between May 21, 2026 and legal close of the Vestas asset acquisitions. The assessment must account for the confirmed fact that GE Vernova and Vestas together constitute the majority of TPI revenue, and must express buffer requirements in weeks of forward coverage at each site's current run rate. Completion evidence is a signed assessment report per facility submitted to the COO and shared with the relevant OEM procurement counterpart, with buffer levels logged in the supply risk dashboard.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation anchors the continuity assessment to the specific equity-transfer window created by the May 21, 2026 confirmation and the pending Vestas acquisition close, replacing a generic buffer calculation with one timed to the actual gap period during which TPI's operational continuity is most uncertain.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Activate formal business continuity procedures for wind blade
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: No later than June 20, 2026 (30 days after the May 21, 2026 plan confirmation), the Business Continuity Manager must activate formal wind blade supply continuity procedures specifically covering the gap period between court confirmation and legal close of Vestas' acquisition of TPI Mexico V LLC and TPI Mexico VI LLC equity, including: (a) a daily operational status check from each of those two facilities, (b) an escalation protocol triggered if either facility reports a production interruption exceeding 48 hours, and (c) a pre-agreed communication cadence with Vestas procurement and GE Vernova supply chain leads. Procedures must remain active and formally reviewed weekly until Vestas acquisition close is legally confirmed, at which point the Business Continuity Manager files a closure memo with the Risk Committee. Completion evidence is the dated activation record, the first weekly status report, and the documented escalation contact list for both OEMs.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation targets the precise legal limbo window between the May 21, 2026 court confirmation and actual equity transfer close for the Mexico assets — the interval during which operational responsibility is transitioning but no new owner has legal authority — a gap not addressed by a generic business continuity activation.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Map all TPI Composites sub-tier material suppliers who
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 30 days of the May 21, 2026 plan confirmation, the Supply Chain Risk Manager must produce a complete map of all TPI Composites sub-tier material suppliers who received advance payments under the December 10, 2025 Vestas Vendor Advance Agreement — the emergency facility approved to address TPI's acute liquidity needs for materials ordered between the petition date and December 5, 2025 — identifying for each sub-tier supplier: (a) total advance amount received, (b) current payment status under the confirmed plan, (c) whether a default risk remains during plan execution, and (d) the OEM blade production lines at Iowa, Arizona, Mexico V, Mexico VI, or India that depend on that supplier's continued delivery. The completed map must be reviewed by the CPO and shared with Vestas and GE Vernova supply chain leads as applicable. Completion evidence is the signed sub-tier supplier map with payment-status columns, filed in the case 25-34655 contract register.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation addresses the confirmed fact that TPI's inability to pay sub-tier suppliers under the Vendor Advance Agreement was acute enough to require emergency court approval, meaning plan-execution-phase sub-tier defaults represent a known residual risk that the generic OPS-018 mapping obligation does not specifically resolve.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Quantify the blade delivery shortfall risk for each
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 60 days of the legal close of the Vestas equity acquisition of TPI Mexico V LLC and TPI Mexico VI LLC (the confirmed plan's primary equity-transfer event), the Demand Planning Director must deliver a quantified blade delivery shortfall analysis for each OEM customer — Vestas and GE Vernova — modelling the scenario in which any single TPI Composites facility (Iowa, Arizona, Mexico V, Mexico VI, or India) fails to resume normal output within 60 days of that closing date; the analysis must express shortfall in number of blades per turbine model, revenue-at-risk per OEM, and the number of turbine delivery dates at risk of breach given that GE Vernova and Vestas together account for the majority of TPI revenue. Results must be presented to the COO and shared with each affected OEM's supply chain executive, with agreed mitigation actions documented. Completion evidence is the signed shortfall report with per-facility, per-OEM scenario tables and countersigned OEM acknowledgement.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation quantifies shortfall risk at the individual facility level against the specific 60-day post-equity-close window defined in the confirmed plan, replacing a generic supply-disruption estimate with an OEM-specific model grounded in TPI's confirmed two-customer revenue concentration.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Disclose in Q2 2026 financial reporting all financial
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: In the Q2 2026 financial statements (covering the period ending June 30, 2026), the CFO must ensure that the notes to financial statements and any related MD&A section include a dedicated TPI Composites disclosure that separately itemises: (a) total amounts advanced or committed under the December 10, 2025 Vestas Vendor Advance Agreement, including maximum contingent exposure; (b) any amounts committed under the GE Renewables North America LLC Long Lead Materials Agreement; (c) any DIP facility exposure; and (d) the recovery assumption applied to each instrument in light of the confirmed Chapter 11 plan's creditor waterfall, including Oaktree Capital's $18.275M uptier settlement priority over unsecured claims. The disclosure must be reviewed by external auditors before filing and must reference case 25-34655 (CML). Completion evidence is the filed Q2 2026 financial statement with the TPI-specific note and auditor sign-off documented in the audit file.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation enforces instrument-level granularity — distinguishing Vendor Advance Agreements, Long Lead Materials obligations, and DIP exposures — and requires recovery assumptions to be benchmarked against the confirmed plan's specific creditor waterfall, which the generic disclosure obligation does not require.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Stress-test OEM counterparty (Vestas
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 45 days of the May 21, 2026 plan confirmation, the Treasury Risk Manager must complete a stress test of all Vestas Wind Systems and GE Vernova receivables and advance-payment recoveries held on the balance sheet, applying the confirmed plan's creditor waterfall as the recovery ceiling — specifically modelling Oaktree Capital Management's $18.275M uptier settlement as a senior priority claim that reduces the pool available to unsecured and trade creditors — and presenting three recovery scenarios (base, downside, and severe) to the CFO and Audit Committee. The stress test must explicitly address whether any Vendor Advance Agreement reimbursement or Long Lead Materials Agreement prepayment recovery is subordinated to Oaktree's priority claim. Completion evidence is the signed stress-test report with scenario tables and Audit Committee acknowledgement recorded in meeting minutes.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation anchors the stress test to the confirmed plan's specific $18.275M Oaktree uptier settlement priority — a concrete, case-confirmed structural feature that directly subordinates trade and advance-payment recoveries — rather than a generic counterparty credit stress scenario.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Evaluate whether the Vestas acquisition of TPI Mexico V
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 90 days of legal close of the Vestas acquisition of TPI Mexico V LLC, TPI Mexico VI LLC, and the India assets, the Chief Strategy Officer must deliver to the board a written reassessment of the OEM vertical-integration policy, evaluating whether Vestas' decision to acquire these TPI facilities — made in the context of a bankruptcy auction at which no qualified third-party bidder emerged in December 2025 — constitutes a structural shift in the independent blade manufacturing market that warrants the acquiring OEM reconsidering its outsourcing policy for remaining non-acquired TPI facilities (Iowa, Arizona). The reassessment must assess whether the absence of any qualified independent bidder at the December 2025 TPI auction signals a market failure in third-party blade supply and what strategic alternatives exist, including partial vertical integration, joint ventures, or long-term capacity reservations with alternative manufacturers. Completion evidence is the signed board paper with a documented board resolution on whether current vertical-integration policy requires amendment.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation grounds the strategic reassessment in the confirmed absence of any qualified third-party bidder at the December 2025 TPI auction — a specific market-structure signal that the generic make-vs-buy framework does not capture — forcing the evaluation to address what that absence implies for long-term OEM supply-chain independence.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Review the wind blade outsourcing strategy in light
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within six months of the legal close of the Vestas acquisition of TPI Mexico V LLC, TPI Mexico VI LLC, and India assets (which represents the primary plan-closing event), the Chief Strategy Officer must present to the board a formal make-vs-buy reassessment for wind blade manufacturing capacity, addressing: (a) the strategic implications of TPI Composites' insolvency as the world's largest independent blade manufacturer; (b) the confirmed absence of any qualified independent bidder at the December 2025 TPI auction; (c) the continued exposure represented by the Iowa and Arizona facilities, which remain outside the Vestas acquisition perimeter; and (d) the impact of the July 4, 2025 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act' PTC construction deadline on near-term blade demand and the urgency of securing supply capacity. The reassessment must include a quantified cost-benefit comparison of at least two alternatives to full outsourcing. Completion evidence is the signed board paper, board resolution, and any approved follow-on workplan filed with the Corporate Secretary.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation specifies that the make-vs-buy clock runs from the actual Vestas acquisition close rather than plan confirmation, and requires the analysis to integrate the PTC construction deadline's demand-acceleration effect — a confirmed regulatory factor that creates urgency the generic outsourcing strategy review does not address.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Confirm that all commercial transactions with TPI Composites
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 20 business days of the May 21, 2026 plan confirmation, the General Counsel must produce and file a compliance certification confirming that each commercial transaction with TPI Composites executed after August 11, 2025 — specifically including (a) the December 10, 2025 Vestas Vendor Advance Agreement, (b) the GE Vernova Long Lead Materials Agreement with GE Renewables North America LLC, and (c) any equity acquisition documents for TPI Mexico V LLC, TPI Mexico VI LLC, and the India assets — was either (i) executed in compliance with the automatic stay provisions of 11 U.S.C. § 362, (ii) authorised by the Southern District of Texas court under Section 363(f) sale authority in case 25-34655 (CML), or (iii) covered by a documented court order or stipulation. Any transaction not fully covered must be escalated to outside bankruptcy counsel for remediation before the equity-transfer close. Completion evidence is the signed certification matrix with court-order citations per transaction, filed in the legal risk register.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation requires transaction-by-transaction automatic-stay and § 363(f) compliance mapping for every post-petition agreement — including the emergency Vendor Advance Agreement whose urgency created heightened risk of procedural non-compliance — rather than a general policy attestation.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Monitor and report on the Production Tax Credit
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Effective immediately, the Regulatory Affairs Director must establish a monthly monitoring report on the Production Tax Credit 'beginning of construction' rule changes enacted under the 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act' signed July 4, 2025 — which requires wind projects to begin construction by July 4, 2026 or be completed by end of 2027 to qualify for the PTC — and must quantify in each report the expected change in blade order volumes from Vestas and GE Vernova (TPI's two confirmed primary customers) attributable to the construction-start deadline, including any orders that may be accelerated, deferred, or cancelled during the post-confirmation period. Reports must be routed monthly to the CFO and Chief Supply Chain Officer and must flag any OEM order-volume change exceeding 10% as a trigger for immediate supply planning review. Completion evidence is the monthly report series with OEM volume-impact tables, filed in the regulatory risk register beginning no later than 30 days after the May 21, 2026 plan confirmation.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: This adaptation directly links the PTC 'beginning of construction' July 4, 2026 hard deadline to TPI's confirmed two-customer revenue concentration — Vestas and GE Vernova — making the monitoring obligation forward-looking to OEM demand shifts during the precise post-confirmation window when TPI's production capacity is most fragile.

incidents.headline: Warrior Technologies oilfield services vendor files Chapter 11 Texas
incidents.summary: On May 21, 2026, Warrior Technologies LLC, a Midland, Texas-based oilfield and industrial services contractor operating across the Permian Basin, filed for Chapter 11 protection in the U.S. Bankruptcy Court for the Southern District of Texas with estimated liabilities of USD 50–100 million against assets of only USD 10–50 million. The company blamed rising fuel and insurance costs for its distress; a Texas judge granted interim approval of USD 9.5 million in DIP financing on May 22, 2026. Warrior Technologies provides hydro excavation, industrial cleaning, oilfield trucking, pipeline support, and site remediation to upstream, midstream, and downstream energy operators as well as government and food-processing clients.

secondary_mappings.why: Warrior Technologies provides critical oilfield services (hydro excavation, pipeline support, remediation) to Permian Basin energy operators; its Chapter 11 threatens service continuity for upstream/midstream customers.
Chapter 11 filing with liabilities exceeding assets by up to USD 90 million creates direct financial exposure for unsecured trade creditors and contract counterparties.
Continuity of hazmat, remediation, and pipeline support services in the Permian Basin depends on resolution of the bankruptcy and availability of DIP financing.

incidents.severity_rationale: Mid-market regional vendor with USD 50–100m liabilities; supply disruption risk is localised to Permian Basin energy operators with likely substitute vendors available in the oilfield services market.
incidents.financial_impact_disclosed: Liabilities USD 50–100 million; assets USD 10–50 million; USD 38 million in secured debt; USD 9.5 million DIP approved

sources.title: Industrial Services Co. Warrior Technologies Hits Ch. 11
Warrior Technologies Chapter 11 Filing Alert
Major Oilfield Services Contractor Files for Chapter 11 Bankruptcy

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure critical operational dependencies on third-party oilfield and industrial services vendors are identified, assessed, and backed by continuity plans sufficient to survive a vendor Chapter 11 filing.

Maintain continuous visibility into the financial health of Tier-1 and Tier-2 vendors operating in concentrated geographies such as the Permian Basin to detect insolvency signals before service disruption occurs.

Ensure operational supply chains for hydro-excavation, industrial cleaning, and oilfield trucking are diversified so that a single-vendor Chapter 11 does not create an unrecoverable gap in Permian Basin field operations.

Ensure financial counterparty exposures, including vendor prepayments, retainers, and contract deposits held with insolvent vendors, are tracked and subject to loss provisioning within the reporting cycle in which insolvency is disclosed.

Maintain tested contingency plans for substitution of oilfield services vendors that have filed for bankruptcy protection and whose continuity as a going concern is uncertain under DIP-financed reorganization.

Establish minimum-redundancy requirements for specialist services — including hydro excavation and pipeline support — where only a small number of vendors operate in a single basin, reducing the impact of a single-vendor failure.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Within 10 business days of Warrior Technologies' May
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By June 4, 2026 (10 business days after the May 21, 2026 petition date in case 26-33562), the Vendor Management Office must produce a complete inventory of every active purchase order, master service agreement, and individual work order issued to Warrior Technologies LLC, with each line item annotated for dollar value, service category (hydro excavation, oilfield trucking, pipeline support, industrial cleaning, or site remediation), current performance status, and remaining committed spend. The resulting register must be reviewed and signed off by the Chief Procurement Officer, and a summary of total committed spend at risk must be delivered to the CFO and General Counsel no later than 5:00 PM CT on June 4, 2026, to inform any decisions on payment holds, contract novation, or DIP-period service continuity.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires contract identification; this adaptation anchors the deadline precisely to the May 21 petition date, mandates service-category tagging aligned to Warrior Technologies' specific service lines, and names the exact approval chain required before the first DIP-period court hearing window closes.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Conduct an enhanced financial-health review of all Permian
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 20 business days of June 4, 2026, the Third-Party Risk team must run an enhanced financial-health screen on all Permian Basin oilfield and industrial services vendors currently providing hydro excavation, oilfield trucking, pipeline support, or site remediation services, applying a distress threshold of a liability-to-asset ratio exceeding 2:1 — calibrated directly to Warrior Technologies LLC's reported USD 50–100 million in liabilities against USD 10–50 million in assets — and specifically probing each vendor's exposure to fuel-cost volatility and insurance-premium escalation as the two distress drivers cited in the Warrior Technologies filing. Any vendor whose profile meets or exceeds this threshold must be escalated immediately to the VP of Supply Chain and flagged in the third-party risk register with a 'watch' status, triggering a mandatory 30-day reassessment cycle.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for a financial-health peer review; this adaptation operationalises the specific 2:1 liability-to-asset distress benchmark derived from the confirmed Warrior Technologies figures, targets the precise cost-driver profile (fuel and insurance) cited in the filing, and imposes a concrete escalation and reassessment cadence rather than leaving the review open-ended.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Verify that existing master service agreements with Warrior
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By May 30, 2026, the Legal team must retrieve and review every master service agreement in force with Warrior Technologies LLC as of the May 21, 2026 petition date and produce a written legal opinion confirming whether each agreement contains (a) an assignment clause that remains enforceable against a debtor-in-possession under 11 U.S.C. § 365 as applied in the Southern District of Texas, and (b) a step-in right that the counterparty may exercise without triggering automatic-stay violations under case 26-33562. Where an agreement is silent on either right or where enforceability is uncertain under Southern District of Texas precedent, Legal must issue a risk flag to the Chief Legal Officer and the Contract Manager no later than May 30, 2026, with a recommended remediation action — such as filing a motion to assume or reject, or negotiating a debtor-in-possession consent — before the next scheduled court date.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires assignment and step-in right verification; this adaptation specifies the governing legal framework (11 U.S.C. § 365, Southern District of Texas practice in case 26-33562), sets a hard deadline ahead of the next court hearing, and mandates a concrete decision pathway for agreements found to be deficient.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Activate alternative-vendor qualification for hydro-excavation and industrial-cleaning capacity
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By June 20, 2026 (30 calendar days from the May 21, 2026 petition date), the Operations and Procurement teams must jointly complete qualification of a minimum of two alternative Permian Basin vendors capable of absorbing Warrior Technologies LLC's contracted volumes in hydro excavation and industrial cleaning, including documented confirmation that each candidate holds the required equipment capacity, insurance coverage, and applicable OSHA and TCEQ certifications to operate in the Permian Basin. Qualification evidence — including completed vendor scorecards, insurance certificates, and a signed letter of intent or framework agreement — must be submitted to the VP of Operations for approval no later than June 20, 2026, with a contingency activation plan specifying how each substitute vendor would be onboarded within 72 hours of a Warrior Technologies service disruption.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires alternative-vendor activation within 30 days; this adaptation ties the deadline explicitly to the May 21 petition date, mandates hydro-excavation and industrial-cleaning specificity consistent with Warrior Technologies' confirmed service profile, and requires a 72-hour activation plan as a bridging mechanism to MC-OPS-029 and MC-OPS-030.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Assess whether any prepayments
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By June 4, 2026, the Finance and Legal teams must jointly identify every prepayment, security deposit, retainer, or advance held by Warrior Technologies LLC as of May 21, 2026 (the petition date in case 26-33562), classify each as a secured or unsecured claim under the debtor-in-possession structure given the reported USD 38 million in senior secured debt, and produce a written recovery-risk memo quantifying expected recovery under both a reorganization scenario (in which the USD 18 million DIP revolving facility and USD 5.7 million DIP term loan are fully drawn) and a liquidation scenario. The memo must be reviewed by the CFO and General Counsel by June 5, 2026, and must include a recommendation on whether to file a proof of claim, participate in the creditors' committee, or seek adequate-protection arrangements for any material unrecovered balance.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires prepayment risk assessment; this adaptation grounds the recovery-risk analysis in the confirmed capital structure — the reported USD 38 million secured debt and the approved DIP package — and sets the June 5 deadline to align with the MC-FIN-029 payment-withholding decision point, ensuring both controls inform each other before cash movements are made.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Update the third-party risk register to reflect Warrior
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By May 28, 2026, the Third-Party Risk Manager must update the enterprise third-party risk register to record Warrior Technologies LLC's Chapter 11 status under case 26-33562, tag each of the five confirmed service lines — hydro excavation, oilfield trucking, pipeline support, industrial cleaning, and site remediation — as 'continuity at risk — DIP interim only,' and note that current operational authority is limited to the USD 9.5 million interim DIP order issued May 22, 2026, pending full court approval of the USD 18 million revolving and USD 5.7 million term-loan DIP package. The register entry must trigger automated risk-tier escalation to 'Critical' and generate weekly status review tasks assigned to the VP of Supply Chain until a final DIP order is entered or the case is converted or dismissed.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires risk-register updating; this adaptation specifies all five confirmed Warrior Technologies service lines, anchors the continuity-risk flag precisely to the gap between the USD 9.5 million interim order and the full DIP package not yet approved, and mandates an automated escalation cadence rather than a one-time update.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Confirm that backup hydro-excavation and industrial-cleaning vendors have
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By June 5, 2026 — within the interim DIP window established by the May 22, 2026 order in case 26-33562 and before Warrior Technologies LLC's next scheduled court hearing — the Operations Director must confirm in writing that at least two alternative hydro-excavation and industrial-cleaning vendors identified under AC-TPR-011-CH11-01 are fully pre-qualified, have executed framework or standby agreements, and can mobilise equipment and certified personnel to active Permian Basin work sites within 24 hours of a written activation notice. Confirmation must include each vendor's available equipment count, personnel certification status, and a signed mobilisation commitment letter, and must be filed with the VP of Operations and Chief Risk Officer as documented evidence of operational contingency readiness.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires backup-vendor pre-qualification; this adaptation locks the confirmation deadline inside the specific interim DIP window created by the May 22 court order, ensuring readiness is documented before the next hearing that will determine whether Warrior Technologies' operational continuity is sustained or disrupted.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Stress-test Permian Basin field-operations schedules against a scenario
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By May 30, 2026, the Operations Planning team must execute a tabletop stress-test of all Permian Basin upstream and midstream field-operations schedules against a scenario in which Warrior Technologies LLC ceases all field operations without notice during the Chapter 11 reorganisation period (case 26-33562), explicitly identifying every work order that would become unserviceable within 72 hours of cessation, ranked by consequence severity (environmental permit obligation, safety-critical gas-mitigation task, or contractual milestone). The stress-test output must be a written gap report delivered to the VP of Operations and the Chief Risk Officer by May 30, 2026, with each unserviceable work order annotated with its required response time, the nearest qualified substitute vendor, and any regulatory notification obligation triggered by a service gap.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a cessation stress-test; this adaptation specifies the 72-hour unserviceability window, mandates consequence-severity ranking aligned to Warrior Technologies' confirmed service categories, and requires regulatory notification obligations to be identified — directly feeding into the environmental-permit continuity obligations addressed in AC-OPS-002-CH11-01.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Determine
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By June 5, 2026, the Controller and General Counsel must jointly review all outstanding invoices owed to Warrior Technologies LLC as of the May 21, 2026 petition date in case 26-33562 and issue a written payment guidance memo determining, for each invoice, whether payment should be withheld pending court clarification of DIP lender priority under the interim USD 9.5 million DIP order, deferred until the full DIP order is entered, or released with documented justification that payment does not constitute a voidable preference under 11 U.S.C. § 547 given the 90-day lookback period. The memo must be approved by the CFO by June 5, 2026, and Accounts Payable must be instructed in writing not to release any payment to Warrior Technologies LLC above an agreed de minimis threshold without explicit CFO sign-off until a final DIP order is entered or Legal advises otherwise.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a payment-withholding assessment; this adaptation applies the specific preference-payment lookback period under 11 U.S.C. § 547, anchors the decision to the confirmed interim DIP order amount and the pending full DIP package in case 26-33562, and establishes a named CFO approval gate to prevent inadvertent preference payments during the reorganisation.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Review business-continuity plans to confirm that the loss
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By May 28, 2026, the Environmental Compliance Manager must review all active environmental permits and remediation work orders currently being serviced by Warrior Technologies LLC in the Permian Basin and produce a written continuity-risk assessment identifying any permit condition or TCEQ-mandated remediation schedule that would be breached if Warrior Technologies ceases site-remediation or hydro-excavation operations during the Chapter 11 reorganisation period under case 26-33562. For each permit at risk, the assessment must specify the regulatory notification requirement, the breach timeline, and the name of the pre-qualified substitute vendor from AC-TPR-011-CH11-01 who can assume the remediation obligation; the assessment must be submitted to the VP of Operations, the Chief Risk Officer, and Legal by May 28, 2026, with a recommended contingency activation trigger.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires BCP review for permit compliance; this adaptation grounds the obligation in Warrior Technologies' specific confirmed service lines (site remediation and hydro excavation), names the TCEQ as the governing regulatory body for Permian Basin operations, and integrates the output directly with the substitute-vendor qualification effort to create an actionable regulatory safeguard rather than a standalone review.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Validate that the roster of Warrior Technologies field
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By June 4, 2026, the HSE Manager and Operations Director must jointly compile a roster of all Warrior Technologies LLC field technicians currently assigned to OSHA-regulated hydro-excavation tasks and gas-mitigation activities on Permian Basin sites, confirm the current certification status of each technician (including OSHA 10/30, confined-space entry, and any DOT or TCEQ-required credentials), and determine whether each pre-qualified substitute vendor identified under AC-TPR-011-CH11-01 has a sufficient pool of equivalently certified personnel to absorb those task assignments without a certification gap. Where a certification gap is identified, the HSE Manager must document a continuity-of-certification plan — including the name of the relevant certifying body, the time required to transfer or re-certify, and any interim supervision arrangement — and submit it to the VP of Operations and the Chief Safety Officer for approval by June 4, 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires certification continuity validation; this adaptation names the specific OSHA and regulatory certification types relevant to Warrior Technologies' confirmed hydro-excavation and gas-mitigation work, ties the roster review to the substitute-vendor pool identified in AC-TPR-011-CH11-01, and requires a named certification-gap remediation plan rather than a general assurance.

incidents.headline: US conducts 'self-defense strikes' on Iran amid Hormuz ceasefire negotiations
incidents.summary: On 25 May 2026, US Central Command announced it conducted 'self-defense strikes' targeting Iranian missile launch sites and boats attempting to emplace mines around the Strait of Hormuz, even as a Pakistan-brokered ceasefire nominally remained in effect. The strikes occurred while US and Iranian negotiators were simultaneously engaged in Qatar-mediated talks toward a memorandum of understanding that would reopen the Strait and provide phased sanctions relief. Key sticking points — Iran's nuclear enrichment programme and the timing of sanctions lifting — remained unresolved, with disputes over language holding up a final deal as of 26 May 2026.

secondary_mappings.why: Sanctions relief and the unfreezing of Iranian assets are the direct quid pro quo being negotiated in parallel with the military standoff, making sanctions exposure a live operational variable.
The Strait of Hormuz closure continues to disrupt global oil, gas, and commodities flows; renewed strikes raise the risk of further supply-chain rupture.
US sanctions on Iran remain in full effect and are being actively used as a financial lever in negotiations; any change in status has immediate balance-sheet implications for affected entities.
Multinational energy, shipping, and financial firms face acute stakeholder and reputational pressure as the conflict's economic impact cascades through commodity markets.

incidents.severity_rationale: Active military exchanges at a choke point handling ~20% of global seaborne oil during ongoing ceasefire; a deal failure risks full conflict resumption and macro energy shock.
incidents.financial_impact_disclosed: US operational cost estimate of $29 billion (Pentagon, May 12 testimony); global oil-export disruption of ~60% from pre-war levels reported

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure the organisation has documented protocols to monitor and respond to armed conflicts at critical maritime chokepoints — specifically the Strait of Hormuz — that directly interrupt operational or supply-chain continuity.

Ensure the organisation maintains a current assessment of sanctions exposure under US OFAC and secondary regimes that may be reshaped by Qatar-mediated MOU negotiations involving phased Iran sanctions relief.

Ensure operational continuity plans address Strait of Hormuz transit disruption as a named single-point-of-failure for energy and container supply chains, with tested alternative routing and force majeure invocation procedures.

Ensure financial risk disclosures and reserve adequacy reviews account for sustained Hormuz closure impacts on energy input costs, war-risk insurance premiums, and sanctions-related counterparty exposures.

Ensure stakeholder communications distinguish between the organisation's direct Hormuz transit exposure and indirect energy-price exposure, preventing conflation that could damage market confidence or trigger regulatory scrutiny.
